serving.natural_language_portion: One standard beef burger with fries set meal, approximately 350 g, comprising a beef patty in a bun with condiments and a regular portion of deep-fried potato fries.

nutrient_source.title: Beef burger with fries — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F08020003 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All core per_100g values are taken from this source without modification. Saturated fat and trans fat supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy closest analogue (fast-food hamburger with condiments and fries) and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef burger with fries as a composite meal in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate components: white burger bun (GI ~70, analogous to white bread rolls per University of Sydney GI Database) and deep-fried potato chips (GI ~63 per University of Sydney GI Database entry for french fries). Weighted composite estimate yields approximately GI 65. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI 65 × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 20.43 × 3.5) / 100 ≈ 29.5 g per 350 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 466 mg/100 g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,631 mg sodium — over 70% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit beef burger with fries; if consumed, request no added salt on fries, omit cheese and ketchup, and compensate with a low-sodium diet for the remainder of the day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI composite meal (estimated GI 65) with 20.4 g carbohydrate/100 g from refined white bun and deep-fried potato; trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g) worsens insulin resistance.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this meal or substitute the white bun with a wholemeal bun and replace deep-fried fries with a side salad or baked potato to reduce glycaemic load and trans fat exposure.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 15.96 g/100 g including saturated fat (~5.2 g/100 g) and trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g); high sodium 466 mg/100 g; deep-frying generates oxidised lipids and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote atherosclerosis.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid deep-fried beef burger meals; if consumed occasionally, choose a grilled beef patty, omit cheese, request no added salt, and replace fries with a non-fried vegetable side to reduce saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g) from deep-frying is a hard pro-inflammatory disqualifier; arachidonic acid from red beef patty amplifies RA inflammatory pathways; AGEs from deep-frying worsen joint inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this meal; trans fat and AGEs from deep-frying are strongly pro-inflammatory, and the beef patty provides arachidonic acid — replace with grilled fish or a plant-based patty and steamed vegetables.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Burger with Fries is a Western fast-food set meal consisting of a grilled or pan-fried beef patty served in a white wheat bun with condiments such as ketchup, mayonnaise, mustard, lettuce, tomato, and cheese, accompanied by a portion of deep-fried potato fries. The beef patty provides savoury, umami-rich flavour with a juicy texture, while the fries are crispy on the outside and soft inside. The bun is soft and slightly sweet. Per 100 g, the meal provides 268 kcal, 10.64 g protein, 15.96 g fat, and 20.43 g carbohydrate, with 466 mg sodium and 2.29 g dietary fibre. The high sodium content (466 mg/100 g) makes this meal unsuitable for individuals with hypertension. The refined carbohydrates from the white bun and deep-fried fries, combined with an estimated GI of 65 and trans fat from frying, make it unsuitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes and cardiovascular disease. Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and oxidised lipids that are pro-inflammatory. In Singapore, this meal is commonly consumed as a working lunch or casual dining option and is widely available at fast-food chains and Western casual restaurants. The meal is tagged as halal in the HPB Singapore Foodo database.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full RESULT JSON from input, populated all mandatory sections, added ontology anchors, GI estimate, health context, and LLM description.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match_type values from 'close' to 'closest_parent'. Ensured -1 sentinel values were referenced in notes field.

Iteration 3: Expanded notes field to individually document every -1 sentinel value in per_100g with specific reason (not reported in HPB Foodo F08020003) and source consulted (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy) for each field, satisfying g3_numeric_hygiene hard gate requirement per VERITAS_SCORING_MODEL_v2.md and the negative sentinel values critical rule.
